1: ginesthe oun mimētai tou theou — mimētai (imitators): the noun appears in Paul also at 1 Cor 4:16; 11:1; Phil 3:17 and 1 Thess 1:6; 2:14. The command to imitate God is unusual — Greek philosophical tradition could speak of assimilation to God (homoiōsis theō, Plato, Theaetetus 176b) but typically as an intellectual-mystical ascent; Paul grounds it in the concrete historical act of Christ's love (v2) and in the relational identity of "beloved children" (tekna agapēta). The oun (therefore) links the exhortation to the preceding forgiveness command (4:32) through the logic: as God forgave you in Christ, become imitators of God.
2:
osmē euōdias — "fragrant aroma" (literally: smell of good aroma): the LXX technical term for the pleasing-aroma of burnt offerings (reah nichoach in MT), used throughout Leviticus (1:9, 13, 17; 2:2, 9, 12; 3:5, etc.) for the acceptable sacrifice. Paul uses the same phrase for the Philippians' gift (Phil 4:18) and for the believer's witness (2 Cor 2:15, osmē). The application of the sacrificial terminus technicus to Christ's self-giving places the cross as the definitive sacrifice that all prior offerings anticipated.

prosphoran kai thysian — "offering and sacrifice": prosphora is the broader term for any offering brought to God; thysia specifically the sacrifice/slaughtered victim. The pairing covers the entire sacrificial repertoire; Ps 40:6 LXX uses both (thysian kai prosophoron ouk ēthelēsas) in the context of the obedient servant whose body-offering supersedes the animal system.
3: porneia kai pasa akatharsia ē pleonexia — "sexual immorality and every impurity or covetousness": the triad will recur in v5 where pleonexia (covetousness) is identified as eidōlolatria (idolatry). The equation of covetousness with idolatry (also Col 3:5) follows the logic that the covetous person worships created goods rather than the Creator. Mēde onomazesthō en hymin — "let it not even be named among you": the prohibition goes beyond non-practice to non-speech — the very naming of these things is inappropriate for the holy community, echoing Levitical purity concerns about the contaminating effect of even associating with the impure.
4:
aischrotēs kai mōrologia ē eutrapelia — "obscenity, foolish talk, or crude joking": eutrapelia (wit, clever talking, jesting) was a positive virtue in Aristotle's ethics (NE 1127b-1128a: the well-bred person who seasons social conversation with apt humor). Paul uses it negatively — not wit per se but the specifically sexual or degrading wit that the context implies. The shift from action to speech (after the action-prohibitions of v3) indicates that the moral formation Paul describes extends from deed to language.

alla mallon eucharistia — "but instead thanksgiving" (eucharistia): the antidote to corrupt speech is not merely silence but grateful speech directed toward God. Thanksgiving (eucharistia) reframes the use of language: instead of speech that degrades, speech that glorifies.
5: pornos ē akathartos ē pleonektēs, ho estin eidōlolatrēs — the appositive "who is an idolater" (ho estin eidōlolatrēs) is grammatically singular but applies to all three types. The identification of the covetous person specifically as the idolater (not the sexually immoral or the impure) follows because pleonexia is desire for more — an idolatrous relationship to possessions that replaces God with goods. Ouk echei klēronomian en tē basileia tou Christou kai theou — "has no inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and of God": the single governing noun phrase with a single article suggests "the kingdom of Christ-and-God" — a high Christological formulation linking Christ and God as co-rulers of the one kingdom.
8: ēte gar pote skotos, nyn de phōs en kyriō — "for you were once darkness, now you are light in the Lord": the radical ontological formulation — not "you were in darkness" but "you were darkness"; not "you are in light" but "you are light." The participatory-ontological language (en kyriō, in the Lord) grounds the identity transformation: the new identity is not achieved but received through union with the one who is light (cf. John 8:12). The past-tense ēte (were) and present-tense este (implied: are) mark the temporal boundary of conversion.
9: ho gar karpos tou phōtos — "for the fruit of the light": some manuscripts read karpos tou pneumatos (fruit of the Spirit, as in Gal 5:22) — a scribal harmonization; the best textual tradition reads phōtos (light). The organic metaphor of fruit distinguishes Spirit/light-produced morality from law-compelled behavior: fruit grows from the life of the tree, not from external constraint. The triad agathōsynē kai dikaiosynē kai alētheia (goodness, righteousness, truth) are the characteristic qualities of the light-community.
10: dokimazein ti estin euareston tō kyriō — "discerning what is pleasing to the Lord" (dokimazein): the testing-and-approving verb that Paul uses for moral discernment throughout (Rom 12:2; Phil 1:10; 1 Thess 5:21). The capacity for moral discernment is the practical outworking of the light-identity: those who are light discern what pleases the Lord through attentive, Spirit-formed judgment rather than rule-application.
11: ta erga ta akarpa tou skotous — "the fruitless works of darkness" (akarpa, without fruit, barren): the opposite of the fruit of light (v9). Darkness produces erga (works, deeds) that are by nature akarpa — they generate nothing lasting, no genuine good. Mē synkoinōneite — "do not participate with" (synkoinōneō = to share together in): the syn-compound emphasizes complicity; the command is to have no joint participation in the darkness-deeds, while elengnete de mallon (but rather expose them) requires active counter-action.
14: Dio legei, Egeire, ho katheudōn, kai anasta ek tōn nekrōn, kai epiphausei soi ho Christos — "Therefore it says: Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you": the source formula dio legei (therefore it says) elsewhere introduces Scripture (4:8; 5:14 is the only NT use without clear scriptural antecedent). The fragment is most likely a baptismal hymn that Paul cites as authoritative — the closest verbal parallel is Isa 60:1 LXX (phōtizou phōtizou Ierousalēm hēkei gar sou to phōs kai hē doxa kyriou epi se anatelalen = arise, shine, for your light has come). The three imperatives (wake, arise, Christ-will-shine) follow the analeptic pattern of baptismal awakening — the conversion-to-light experience rendered in poetic form.
15: blepete oun akribōs pōs peripateite — "pay careful attention then to how you walk" (akribōs: precisely, exactly, carefully — the same word for the Magi's careful inquiry about the star in Matt 2:7): the peripateo (walk) metaphor, used throughout Ephesians for the pattern of life (2:2, 10; 4:1, 17; 5:2, 8, 15), is here governed by akribōs — deliberate, watchful attention to the direction of one's life. Mē hōs asophoi all' hōs sophoi — "not as unwise but as wise": the wisdom frame recasts the moral life as a matter of perception and judgment (sophia) rather than rule-compliance.
16: exagorazomenoi ton kairon — "buying back the time, making the most of the opportunity" (exagorazō: to buy out of the marketplace, redeem by paying the price): the commercial metaphor implies seizing the available opportunity before it closes — the kairos (opportune time, moment) is not chronos (measured clock-time) but the specific moment with its unique potential. Col 4:5 uses the identical phrase in the context of behavior toward outsiders. Hoti hai hēmerai ponērai eisin — "because the days are evil": the eschatological pressure grounds the urgency; the present age is evil but time-limited, making every available moment for redemptive action urgent.
17: mē ginesthe aphrōnes, alla syniete ti to thelēma tou kyriou — "do not be foolish, but understand what the will of the Lord is" (syniete: second-person plural imperative of syniēmi, to comprehend through assembly of parts): the wisdom-foolishness polarity (cf. Prov 8-9) governs understanding the divine will. The will of the Lord is not a specific list of behaviors but a pattern of reality to be understood (syniete) through wisdom-formation — the Spirit-filled community discerns it in worship and community life (vv18-21).
18: mē methyskaesthe oinō, en hō estin asōtia, alla plērousthe en pneumati — "do not get drunk with wine — in which is debauchery — but be filled with the Spirit" (asōtia: prodigal, reckless living; same word for the prodigal son's lifestyle in Luke 15:13): the contrast is not between alcohol and abstinence but between two kinds of altered state and two kinds of communal expression — wine-filling produces pagan revelry; Spirit-filling produces the worship described in vv19-21. Plērousthe is a present passive imperative: keep being filled, continuously receive the filling — the ongoing work of the Spirit in the community. En pneumati — "in the Spirit" (instrumental or sphere): the Spirit is both the means and the environment of the filling.
19: psalmois kai hymnois kai ōdais pneumatikais — "in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs" (Col 3:16 uses an identical phrase): the three terms may represent (a) three distinct genres (Psalter/OT psalms, composed Christian hymns, Spirit-improvised songs); or (b) overlapping categories for the full range of community song. Pneumatikais (spiritual) may modify all three nouns (psalms, hymns, songs that are all Spirit-given) rather than only the third. Adōntes kai psallontes tē kardia hymōn tō kyriō — "singing and making melody with your heart to the Lord": psallō originally meant to pluck a string instrument but by the NT period generally meant to sing praise. "With your heart" (tē kardia) — inner participation not only outer performance.
21: hypotassomenoi allēlois en phobō Christou — "submitting to one another in the fear of Christ": the present middle/passive participle hypotassomenoi (submitting, being subject) is syntactically dependent on plērousthe (v18), making mutual submission the fifth result of Spirit-filling (alongside speaking, singing, giving thanks). The allēlois (one another, mutually) is the governing principle: the submission is reciprocal before it is hierarchical. The household code that follows (5:22-6:9) elaborates specific relational dynamics within this mutuality, not as an exception to it but as its concrete application in asymmetric relationships.
22: hai gynaikes tois idiois andrasin hōs tō kyriō — "wives to their own husbands as to the Lord": the verb is absent in the best manuscripts (supplied from v21's hypotassomenoi): "wives [submitting] to your own husbands." The idiois (own) specifies the husband in the particular marriage relationship — not a general female subordination to males but the specific marital relationship. Hōs tō kyriō (as to the Lord): the submission's quality and motive are shaped by the Christ-relationship; the husband functions as a representational analogy of the Lord, not as a substitute lord.
23: hoti anēr estin kephalē tēs gynaikos hōs kai ho Christos kephalē tēs ekklēsias, autos sōtēr tou sōmatos — "because the husband is the head of the wife as also Christ is the head of the church, he himself being the savior of the body": kephalē (head) is debated: (a) source/origin (as in 4:15-16 where the head is the source from which the body grows); (b) authority-over (as in the common OT/Greek usage for rulers). The Christ-analogy determines what headship means: Christ's headship is exercised as savior of the body — a life-giving, self-sacrificial role. Autos sōtēr tou sōmatos (he himself the savior of the body) is the defining qualification: headship in this context is characterized by saving rather than dominating.
25: hoi andres, agapate tas gynaikas, kathōs kai ho Christos ēgapēsen tēn ekklēsian kai heauton paredōken hyper autēs — "husbands, love your wives as also Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her" (paredōken: aorist active of paradidōmi, delivered over, handed over — the same verb used for Judas's betrayal-handover and for God's delivering Jesus to death in Rom 4:25; 8:32): the measure of husband-love is the cross. The self-handover language places the husband's love explicitly in the economy of Christ's atoning self-giving — not a softer version but the full measure.
26: hagiasē autēn katharisas tō loutrō tou hydatos en rhēmati — "having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word/in word" (tō loutrō tou hydatos = the bath/washing of water): loutron (washing, bath) refers to baptism in Titus 3:5; here the washing of water is the instrument of the church's purification. En rhēmati (in/with word) — the accompanying word: either (a) the baptismal confession or formula spoken at baptism; (b) the gospel-word that accompanied the baptismal event; or (c) the word of Scripture. The purpose clause hina autēn hagiasē (that he might sanctify her) identifies the washing as the instrument of sanctification — the eschatological purity of the church is already enacted in the community's baptism.
27: hina parastēsē autos heautō endoxon tēn ekklēsian mē echousan spillon ē rhytida ē ti tōn toioutōn all' hina ē hagia kai amōmos — "that he might present the church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle or any such thing, but so that she might be holy and without blemish": parastēsē (present, place alongside) is the presenting of a bride at the wedding or of a sacrifice at the altar. Endoxon (in glory/splendor) — the eschatological presentation has a radiant quality. The bridal purity vocabulary (spillon = spot/stain; rhytida = wrinkle — NT hapax legomena) describes unblemished beauty. Hagia kai amōmos (holy and without blemish) is the Levitical standard for sacrificial animals applied to the church-as-bride.
31: anti toutou kataleipsei anthrōpos ton patera kai tēn mētera kai proskollēthēsetai pros tēn gynaika autou, kai esontai hoi dyo eis sarka mian — Paul cites Gen 2:24 LXX (with minor variations): anti toutou (therefore/for this reason) begins the citation. The proskollāō (cleave to, be glued/joined to) emphasizes the permanence of the bond. Eis sarka mian (into one flesh) is the ontological union of marriage that Paul will interpret as the mysterion in v32.
32: to mysterion touto mega estin; egō de legō eis Christon kai eis tēn ekklēsian — "this mystery is great; I am speaking with reference to Christ and the church": the demonstrative touto refers back to the Gen 2:24 citation — the mystery is in the ancient text, not simply in the institution of marriage. Paul's interpretive move (egō de legō: I am saying, for my part) applies the one-flesh text to the Christ-church union: the "great mystery" is the deep meaning hidden in the creation ordinance, now disclosed in the gospel. This is the typological hermeneutic at work: the human marriage pattern in creation always already pointed toward its antitype in the covenant union of Christ and the church.
33: plēn kai hymeis hoi kath' hena, hekastos tēn heautou gynaika houtōs agapatō hōs heauton, hē de gynē hina phobētai ton andra — "nevertheless, each one of you is to love his wife as himself, and the wife is to fear/respect her husband" (phobētai: the verb of reverence/fear, the same as phobō Christou in v21): plēn (nevertheless, however) transitions from the theological mysterion to the practical summary. The husband's love-obligation and the wife's respect-obligation are the two asymmetric requirements that flow from the Christ-church analogy. Phobos here is reverential respect, not terror — the same quality that characterizes the community's relation to Christ (v21).
6: mē tis humas apatātō kenois logois — "let no one deceive you with empty words" (kenois logois: hollow, without content — kenos contrasts with the fullness language throughout Ephesians): the deception consists in rationalizing the sins of vv3-5 as inconsequential. huioi tēs apeithias (sons of disobedience) — the identical phrase as 2:2, recalling the pre-conversion existence of all believers under the power of disobedience; the reminder is that these behaviors are not a neutral lifestyle but the defining characteristic of the unregenerate humanity. erchetai hē orgē tou theou — the present-tense erchetai (comes, is coming) treats the wrath of God as already operative in the present age, not merely a future eschatological threat; the wrath falls epi (upon/on account of) these very things.
7: mē oun ginesthe synkoinōnoi autōn — "do not therefore become co-participants with them" (synkoinōnoi: fellow-partakers; the syn-compound makes the complicity explicit — to be a fellow-participant is to share in the act and its consequences): the oun draws the practical consequence from v6. The warning is not merely against practice but against fellowship-in-the-acts: partial involvement, social tolerance of the deeds, looking on without protest. The verse closes the warning section (vv3-7) before the light-identity section opens (v8).
12: ta gar kryptē ginomena hyp' autōn aischron estin kai legein — "for the things done by them in secret it is shameful even to speak of" (kryptē: in hiddenness, in secret; aischron: shameful, dishonorable — the word of social disgrace in the honor-shame register): their deeds require concealment precisely because they cannot bear public scrutiny; this is the negative ground for the exposure command (v11). The verse does not name the specific deeds but gestures toward them — the shame is compounded by description, so the text maintains decorum by refusing to specify. The estin kai legein (it is [shameful] even to speak of) intensifies: not merely doing them but naming them participates in the dishonor.
13: ta de panta elegchomena hypo tou phōtos phaneroutai — "but everything exposed by the light is made visible" (elegchō: to reprove, expose, convict — the word used for the Spirit's convicting work in John 16:8; phaneroo: to make manifest, render visible): the logic: what is reproved by the light does not simply get condemned — it gets made manifest, drawn into visibility. pan gar to phaneromenon phōs estin — "for everything that is made manifest is light" — the most striking claim: exposure by the light is not merely revelation of darkness but transformation into light. The verse grounds the imperative to expose (v11) in this ontological logic: the act of exposure-by-light is itself the act of light-transformation, not merely condemnation.
20: eucharistountes pantote hyper pantōn en onomati tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou tō theō kai patri — "always giving thanks to God the Father for all things in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ": eucharistountes is the fourth present participle in the Spirit-filling sequence of vv18-21 (alongside speaking, singing, making melody, and submitting — v21), not a separate command but a continuous fruit of Spirit-filling. Pantote... hyper pantōn — "always... for all things": the double universality (all times, all circumstances) is the theologically demanding feature; the thanksgiving encompasses what cannot be received as good on its own terms, grounded in the mediation of Christ's name. En onomati tou kyriou hēmōn (in the name of our Lord) — thanksgiving is directed to the Father through the Christ-name, the same mediated access described in 2:18 (di' autou tēn prosagōgēn).
24: alla hōs hē ekklēsia hypotassetai tō Christō, houtōs kai hai gynaikes tois andrasin en panti — "but as the church submits to Christ, so also wives to husbands in everything": hypotassetai (is subject, submits) — present tense, indicating the ongoing reality of the church's submission as the paradigm: not an occasional act but a continuous posture. The alla (but) marks the concessive move: after v23's christological grounding of headship, this verse draws the analogical application. The church's submission is not merely an illustration but the formative model — the wife's submission is characterized by the quality and grounding of the church's submission to Christ. En panti (in everything) — the scope parallels the total self-sacrifice demanded of husbands in v25; the asymmetry is in role, not in totality.
28: houtōs opheilousin kai hoi andres agapan tas heautōn gynaikas hōs ta heautōn sōmata — "in this same way, husbands ought to love their own wives as their own bodies" (opheilousin: ought, are obligated — debt-language; the husband's love is not optional sentiment but moral necessity): the houtōs (in this same way) applies the Christ-church pattern of vv25-27 to the self-care argument: as Christ nourishes the church which is his body, so the husband who loves his wife loves his own body-in-her (since they are one flesh, v31). Ho agapōn tēn heautou gynaika heauton agapā — "he who loves his own wife loves himself": the self-love argument does not sanction selfishness but subverts it — the husband cannot treat his wife as other because she is, in the one-flesh union, himself.
29: oudeis gar pote tēn heautou sarka emisēsen, alla ektrephei kai thalpei autēn — "for no one ever hated his own flesh, but nourishes and cherishes it" (ektrephō: to nourish, bring to maturity — used in 6:4 for raising children; thalpō: to warm, cherish, tend with protective care — Paul uses it in 1 Thess 2:7 for his own maternal nursing of the community): the two verbs together describe attentive bodily care — feeding and warming. Kathōs kai ho Christos tēn ekklēsian — the self-care analogy points beyond itself to Christ's care of the church as the governing instance; the husband's care of his wife is measured by and participates in Christ's care of the body. What Christ does for the church — nourish and cherish — is the template for husband-love, not merely an analogy.
30: hoti mele esmen tou sōmatos autou — "because we are members of his body": the hoti (because) provides the ground for v29's claim that Christ nourishes and cherishes the church — he does so because it is his body, of which believers are members. Mele (members, limbs) — the body-membership language is the foundation for the husband-love argument: as the husband's care for his wife follows from their one-flesh union, Christ's care for the church follows from the believers' incorporation into his body. Some manuscripts add ek tēs sarkos autou kai ek tōn osteōn autou (from his flesh and from his bones), echoing Adam's cry over Eve in Gen 2:23 — if original, it deepens the Adam-Christ/Eve-church typology that the citation of Gen 2:24 (v31) makes explicit.
